title: On structure results for intertwining operators
speaker: Wilhelm Schlag
abstract: The intertwining wave operators are basic objects in the scattering theory of a Hamiltonian given as the sum of a Laplacian with a potential. These Hamiltonians are the classical Schroedinger operators of quantum mechanics. For the three dimensional case we will discuss a new representation of the wave operators as superpositions of reflections and translations. This is joint work with Marius Beceanu, Albany.

proof_stages.label: Classical wave operators and limiting absorption
proof_stages.summary: The talk introduces Schrödinger wave operators as strong limits, records their isometry and intertwining properties, and recalls how classical asymptotic completeness is driven by Fourier trace estimates and the limiting absorption principle.

proof_stages.label: Critical decay and spectral thresholds
proof_stages.summary: The speaker separates the high-frequency short-range x^{-1} threshold from the scaling and zero-energy x^{-2}/L^{d/2} threshold, emphasizing Birman-Schwinger invertibility and the obstructions caused by zero-energy resonances or eigenvalues.

proof_stages.label: Sharpness and random-potential motivation
proof_stages.summary: Sharp anisotropic embedded-eigenvalue examples and random-potential results motivate the search for stronger structure, while self-energy loops and renormalization issues reveal why naive critical estimates fail.

proof_stages.label: Yajima LP theory and the first structural kernel
proof_stages.summary: Yajima's LP-boundedness theory is recast through Duhamel expansions and explicit Fourier/resolvent formulas, leading to a representation of the first wave-operator term as an average of translations and reflections.

proof_stages.label: Full three-dimensional structure theorem
proof_stages.summary: The main structure theorem is stated: under a B_{1+} potential condition and zero-energy regularity in dimension three, the full wave operator equals the identity plus an averaged translation-reflection operator, yielding boundedness on Banach spaces invariant under those operations.

proof_stages.label: Operator Wiener theorem for summing the Dyson series
proof_stages.summary: Classical Wiener theory is upgraded to an operator-valued convolution algebra so that pointwise invertibility of Birman-Schwinger-type Fourier transforms can replace smallness and sum the formally divergent series.

proof_stages.label: Nine-variable ambient algebra and recursive kernels
proof_stages.summary: A correct multi-variable algebra with contraction and star product is built to encode modulated Birman-Schwinger kernels, express the resolvent identity at the wave-operator level, and recursively construct the higher structure functions.

proof_stages.label: Wiener inversion in the new algebra and scaling-critical outlook
proof_stages.summary: An abstract Wiener theorem in the new algebra gives the required inversion and quantitative bounds, after which the speaker explains the current small-potential scaling-invariant result and the goal of extending it to large, time-dependent critical potentials.

examples.name: Jerison-type anisotropic embedded-eigenvalue potential
examples.note: The potential decays anisotropically at the borderline rate and has embedded eigenvalues, showing why the stated decay threshold is sharp rather than merely technical.

examples.name: Square Fermi surface for the two-dimensional discrete Laplacian
examples.note: The discrete Laplacian's level sets can become square or degenerate, illustrating why Bourgain's random-potential theorem had technical energy exclusions.
examples.engagement_reason: notes that the two-dimensional discrete Laplacian has a square/nasty Fermi surface near the spectrum center, without computing it

passes.reason: The middle-energy level is the highly symmetric van-Hove square with flat sides and corners, obstructing a curved round Fermi-surface reduction.
The middle-energy Fermi surface is a flat cornered D4-symmetric van-Hove level obstructing a smooth round reduction.
The central discrete-Laplacian Fermi surface is a van-Hove critical level with flat/singular square geometry obstructing the smooth round-surface reduction.
The band-center Fermi surface is a highly special critical level with flat/corner degeneracies, obstructing the smooth curved-surface reduction.

The central discrete-Laplacian Fermi surface is a critical square/van-Hove level with corners and curvature degeneracy obstructing the round-surface reduction.
the square van-Hove Fermi level obstructs the smooth curved-surface argument and is a symmetric singular critical level

examples.name: N by sqrt(N) rectangle saturating the self-energy loop

examples.engagement_reason: uses the N by sqrt(N) rectangle-supported potential to test the self-energy loop and concludes it gives size one and the l3 numerology
